did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically Inception-v3 and Wide ResNet architectures, trained on the ISIC dermoscopy dataset to perform binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. other, and biopsy vs. follow) in order to quantify the impact of image artefacts on machine learning accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes original experimental work involving the training of Convolutional Neural Networks (Inception-v3 and Wide ResNet) on the ISIC dataset to quantitatively measure the impact of image artefacts on classification accuracy. It presents new results, methods, and conclusions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Quantifying acceptable artefact ranges for dermatologic classification algorithms
brief_summary: This study quantified the impact of various image artefacts on the accuracy of dermatologic classification algorithms using two convolutional neural networks, Inception-v3 and Wide ResNet. The models were trained on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive to perform binary classification tasks for melanoma diagnosis and biopsy recommendation. Artefacts such as blur, hue shifts, and lighting variations were systematically injected into dermoscopy images to evaluate their effect on performance metrics like area under the curve and specificity. The results indicated that general blur had the strongest negative effect on melanoma versus other classification, while shifting hue towards blue significantly impacted the suspicious versus follow task. The authors conclude that classifiers must either mitigate or detect artefacts, and images outside the machine's perceived quality range should be excluded to maintain accuracy and regulatory approval.

lesion_types_studied.details: Positive class in the 'melanoma versus other' diagnosis task (1728 samples in training set) and positive class in the 'biopsy versus follow' management decision task (7092 samples in training set).

Negative class ('other') in the 'melanoma versus other' diagnosis task. Included in the 'follow' group for the management decision task if no biopsy was recommended. Specific counts not provided for individual subtypes in the 'other' group, but total 'other' samples were 2591 in the training set (4319 total - 1728 melanoma).
Listed as one of the diagnosis labels included in the 'other' category for the melanoma vs. other task. Also mentioned as a diagnosis that might still be marked 'biopsy' if a clinician was in doubt.
Listed as a diagnosis that was marked 'biopsy' for the management decision task.
Listed as a diagnosis that was marked 'biopsy' for the management decision task.

lesion_types_studied.role: Included in target class (biopsy) and comparator class (other)
lesion_types_studied.details: Listed as a diagnosis that was marked 'biopsy' for the management decision task. Also listed as one of the diagnosis labels included in the 'other' category for the melanoma vs. other task.

lesion_types_studied.details: Listed as a diagnosis that was marked 'biopsy' for the management decision task. Also listed as one of the diagnosis labels included in the 'other' category for the melanoma vs. other task.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: melanoma vs. other skin lesions, and biopsy vs. follow management decisions.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states it trained two convolutional neural networks (Inception-v3 and Wide ResNet) on two different binary classification tasks. The first task was 'melanoma versus other' (diagnosis), and the second was 'biopsy versus follow' (management decision). The models predicted discrete labels (melanoma/other or biopsy/follow) using binary cross-entropy loss, and performance was measured using Area Under the Curve (AUC) and specificity-at-given-sensitivity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available repository of digitized dermoscopy images with clinical labels. The authors used images from this archive, which originally contained over 23,000 images. They manually cropped images to remove inclusions (hair, ink, rulers) and resize them for CNN input, resulting in specific dataset sizes for their tasks.
classes_used: Other (including actinic keratosis, angioma, atypical melanocytic proliferation, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, lentigo NOS, lentigo simplex, nevus, other, pigmented benign keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, solar lentigo, squamous cell carcinoma, vascular lesion)

use.training: Used to train two convolutional neural networks (Inception-v3 and Wide ResNet-101-2) for binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. other, and biopsy vs. follow). Data was split 80/20 into training/testing sets, with class imbalance compensated by weights. Monte Carlo Cross Validation was performed with 9 iterations.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the impact of artefacts on classification accuracy. Results from nine iterations of Monte Carlo Cross Validation were averaged.
use.other: Pretrained on ImageNet dataset before fine-tuning on the ISIC data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the data randomly (bootstrap with replacement) for training and testing iterations, with results from nine iterations averaged to provide final results.
To compensate for class imbalance in the loss function, ensuring smaller class errors carried more weight.
To report algorithm performance on receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves.
To assess algorithm performance at defined clinical sensitivity operating points (0.84 for diagnostic task, 0.89 for management task).

To establish 'below average' performance thresholds by subtracting the difference between expert and average dermatologist performance (from Marchetti et al. and Haenssle et al.) from control test AUC and specificity values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were made against 'below average' dermatologist thresholds derived from expert vs. average dermatologist differences in prior literature (Marchetti et al. and Haenssle et al.), but no statistical significance testing was performed on the artefact impact results.

ISIC_Diagnosis_Task.description: Binary classification of melanoma versus other skin lesions. Training set size: 4319 images (1728 melanoma, 2591 other). Sensitivity threshold for specificity calculation: 0.84.
ISIC_Diagnosis_Task.notes: General blur had the strongest negative effect on the melanoma versus other task. Wide ResNet was more robust on the management task, whereas Inception-v3 was more robust on the diagnostic task. Motion blur did not have as significant an impact. Saturation had the least impact. Values in bold in the source table represent performance dropping below 'below average' dermatologist thresholds.

ISIC_Management_Decision_Task.description: Binary classification of biopsy versus follow. Training set size: 17729 images (7092 biopsy, 10637 follow). Sensitivity threshold for specificity calculation: 0.89.
ISIC_Management_Decision_Task.notes: Shifting the hue towards blue had a more pronounced effect on the suspicious versus follow task. Wide ResNet was more robust on the management task compared to Inception-v3. Results are averaged from nine iterations of Monte Carlo Cross Validation.

architecture_used.input_size: 299 x 299 pixels (cropped from resized 350 x 350 images)

architecture_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1060 GPU, Linux (Ubuntu 16.04), 16-GB RAM, Intel Core i7-6700K CPU
architecture_details.modification: Final fully connected 1000-class layer replaced with a single fully connected binary layer with random weights

training_details.regularization: Default built-in regularization in the networks
training_details.data_augmentation: Resizing to 350x350, random cropping to 299x299, random horizontal and vertical flipping (50-50 Bernoulli trial), class weights for imbalance compensation
training_details.validation_method: Monte Carlo Cross Validation (9 iterations, bootstrap with replacement)

preprocessing_techniques: Manual cropping of images to remove inclusions (hair, ink, rulers) and retain a roughly square region around the lesion with a margin no greater than the lesion's diameter.
Random cropping of resized images to 299 x 299 pixels to meet Inception-v3 input requirements.
Random horizontal and vertical flipping (50-50 Bernoulli trial) for data augmentation.
Color normalization of image channels according to PyTorch documentation standards during image loading.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were resized down to 350 x 350 pixels.
Random cropping of resized images to 299 x 299 pixels to meet Inception-v3 input requirements.
Random horizontal and vertical flipping using a standard 50-50 Bernoulli trial.
Image color channels were normalized as per PyTorch documentation standards during the image loading process.
Class imbalance was compensated for by using class weights on the loss function such that the smaller class errors carried more weight.

conclusions: Classifiers should either mitigate artefacts or detect them to maintain accuracy.
Images should be excluded from diagnosis or recommendation when artefacts are present in amounts outside the machine's perceived quality range.
Failure to exclude images with excessive artefacts will reduce accuracy and impede approval from regulatory agencies.
General blur had the strongest negative effect on the melanoma versus other classification task.
Shifting the hue towards blue had a more pronounced negative effect on the suspicious versus follow (biopsy vs. follow) task.
Wide ResNet was more robust than Inception-v3 on the management decision task, whereas Inception-v3 was more robust on the diagnostic task.
Motion blur did not have as significant an impact as general blur, implying CNNs are robust to some amount of human shake during image acquisition.
Saturation variations had the least impact of the tested artefacts.
Simple metrics of accuracy are insufficient to describe the usefulness of a classifier due to wide performance variations across images with different impediments.
The study introduces the concept of 'machine perceived image quality' to quantitatively report the impact of different artefact classes on machine learning algorithms.
important_remarks: The study is preliminary due to the relatively small dataset size (4,319 images for diagnosis task), and the authors caution that actual numbers should be interpreted with this limitation in mind.

Artefact simulation parameters were based on expert judgement of MoleMapper™ data rather than validated computer vision techniques, so the quantitative values may not perfectly reflect real-world data distributions.

The study excluded bubble and hair artefacts because they are difficult to simulate realistically or are already well-studied, limiting the generalizability of the findings to all common dermoscopy artefacts.

Performance benchmarks were derived from comparing expert vs. average dermatologist differences in prior literature (Marchetti et al. and Haenssle et al.) rather than direct clinical trials, serving as a proxy for 'below average' dermatologist performance.

The models were trained on the ISIC archive, which contains images from various undocumented camera and dermoscopy equipment, potentially introducing dataset bias regarding colour and lighting variations.
The study did not perform formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values); instead, it relied on comparing performance drops against established dermatologist thresholds.

Simple accuracy metrics are insufficient to describe classifier usefulness because performance varies widely across images with different impediments; the paper advocates for 'machine perceived image quality' ranges.
The research was conducted on cropped images to remove inclusions (hair, ink, rulers) and resize for CNN input, which may not reflect the performance on raw, unprocessed clinical images.
The authors note that failure to exclude images with excessive artefacts could reduce accuracy and impede regulatory approval, highlighting a critical deployment concern for clinical AI.
The study focuses on binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. other, biopsy vs. follow) and may not generalize to multiclass classification or other dermatologic conditions.
